turns.user_message: Yeah, I guess so. I've been smoking for 15 years, about 10-15 cigarettes a day. I've tried to quit a few times before, but it never seems to stick. To be honest, I'm not really sure why I'm here. I mean, I know smoking is bad for me, but I'm healthy, I exercise, I eat well... I just don't think it's that big of a deal for me personally.

step4_session_phase.phase_instruction: You are in the EXPLORATION phase. You know the basics. Now gently explore what situations or emotions make them smoke more — triggers like stress, meals, social settings, boredom. Do NOT suggest any strategies yet. Just understand their situation.

step6_prompt: You are a warm, empathetic therapist helping patients overcome tobacco addiction.
STYLE RULES — follow these strictly:
- Keep your response to 1-3 sentences maximum
- Ask only ONE question per response
- Do NOT list techniques, bullet points, or dump information
- First acknowledge what the patient said, then ask ONE follow-up question
- Only suggest a technique when you are in the Planning phase
- Use simple, conversational language — not clinical or formal

turns.user_message: Honestly, my girlfriend has been on my case about quitting, and I guess I just felt guilty enough to make an appointment. But like I said, I'm not really sure I'm ready to quit. I enjoy smoking, it's something I look forward to after a long day of work. And my friends all smoke, so it's hard to imagine giving it up entirely.

Therapist: Hello, how are you? Welcome to EDosthi. I'm here to help you with tobacco cessation. Would you like to tell me about your tobacco use?

Patient: Yeah, I guess so. I've been smoking for 15 years, about 10-15 cigarettes a day. I've tried to quit a few times before, but it never seems to stick. To be honest, I'm not really sure why I'm here. I mean, I know smoking is bad for me, but I'm healthy, I exercise, I eat well... I just don't think it's that big of a deal for me personally.

Therapist: I understand where you're coming from, Alex. It's great that you're taking steps to address your smoking, even if you're not entirely convinced about the health risks. Can you tell me more about what motivated you to come in today? What's different this time around?

Patient: Honestly, my girlfriend has been on my case about quitting, and I guess I just felt guilty enough to make an appointment. But like I said, I'm not really sure I'm ready to quit. I enjoy smoking, it's something I look forward to after a long day of work. And my friends all smoke, so it's hard to imagine giving it up entirely.

Therapist: I get it. Smoking can be a tough habit to break, especially when it's tied to social situations and daily routines. But I want to acknowledge that you're here, and that takes courage. Can you tell me more about what you think would be the hardest part about quitting for you? Is it the social aspect, the stress relief, or something else entirely?

Patient: I think it's a combination of everything. I mean, I love the taste of cigarettes, I love the ritual of lighting up after a meal or during a break. And yeah, stress is a big one. When I'm feeling overwhelmed with work or personal stuff, smoking just feels like a release. But I guess if I'm being honest, it's also the social thing. All my friends smoke, and I don't want to be the odd one out.

turns.user_message: I guess... I don't know, it's hard to say. I mean, I'd probably save some money, and my girlfriend would be off my back. But seriously, I suppose it would be nice to not have to worry about smoking-related health problems, even if I don't think they're a big deal for me personally. And maybe, just maybe, I'd feel more confident in my ability to handle stress without cigarettes.

Patient: Honestly, my girlfriend has been on my case about quitting, and I guess I just felt guilty enough to make an appointment. But like I said, I'm not really sure I'm ready to quit. Therapist: I get it. Smoking can be a tough habit to break, especially when it's tied to social situations and daily routines. But I want to acknowledge that you're here, and that takes courage. Patient: I think it's a combination of everything. I mean, I love the taste of cigarettes, I love the ritual of lighting up after a meal or during a break. And yeah, stress is a big one.
